questions.q: Which condition distinguishes a Banach space from an arbitrary normed space?
choices: the norm is induced by an inner product
the space is complete in the metric $d(x,y)=\|x-y\|$
questions.explain: A Banach space is a normed vector space in which every Cauchy sequence converges. Inner products give Hilbert spaces; compact unit balls characterize finite-dimensional normed spaces.

questions.q: A norm $\|\cdot\|$ on a real vector space arises from an inner product if and only if it satisfies:
questions.explain: The Jordan–von Neumann theorem: a norm comes from an inner product iff it obeys the parallelogram law. The inner product is then recovered by polarization.

questions.q: In $\ell^2$, compute $\|x\|_2$ for $x=(1,2,2,0,0,\ldots)$.

hard.q: The space $C[0,1]$ with the $L^2$ norm is NOT a Banach space. Which is the correct reason, and which is its completion?
choices: The $L^2$ norm fails the triangle inequality on $C[0,1]$; the completion is $C^1[0,1]$
The sequence $f_n(x)=x^n$ is Cauchy in $L^2$ but its $L^2$ limit is not continuous; the completion is $L^2[0,1]$
There are no Cauchy sequences in $C[0,1]$ under $\|\cdot\|_{L^2}$, so completeness is vacuously false
$C[0,1]$ is already a Banach space under the $L^2$ norm because it is finite-dimensional
hard.explain: The functions $f_n(x)=x^n$ form a Cauchy sequence in $C[0,1]$ under $\|\cdot\|_{L^2}$, but their $L^2$ limit is the indicator $\mathbf{1}_{{1}}$, which is not continuous. Thus $C[0,1]$ is not closed under $L^2$ limits; its metric completion is $L^2[0,1]$.

hard.q: Which is the correct order of steps for proving $\ell^2$ is a Hilbert space via the Riesz–Fischer approach?
choices: Define inner product → check linearity → apply Gram–Schmidt → conclude completeness
Take a Cauchy sequence → extract a fast-Cauchy subsequence with $\|x_{n_{k+1}}-x_{n_k}\|<2^{-k}$ → show coordinatewise limit $x$ lies in $\ell^2$ → verify $\|x_{n_k}-x\|\to 0$ → conclude
Apply Hahn–Banach to extend each coordinate functional → invoke Banach–Alaoglu → extract convergent subsequence
Show the unit ball is compact → deduce completeness from sequential compactness
hard.explain: The standard proof: pass to a fast-Cauchy subsequence (so the telescoping series of norms converges), form the coordinatewise limit $x$, use the dominated convergence-style bound $\sum|x_k|^2\le(\|x_{n_1}\|+\sum 2^{-k})^2<\infty$ to put $x\in\ell^2$, then verify norm convergence. The unit ball of an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space is never compact.

hard.q: Let $e_1,e_2\in\ell^2$ be the first two standard basis vectors. Using the polarization identity $\langle x,y\rangle=\tfrac{1}{4}(\|x+y\|^2-\|x-y\|^2)$ (real case), compute $\langle e_1+2e_2,\, 3e_1-e_2\rangle$.

operator-norm.title: Bounded linear operators and the operator norm

questions.q: The operator norm of $T\in B(X,Y)$ is defined by $\|T\|=\sup_{\|x\|\le 1}\|Tx\|$. Which equivalent expression is INCORRECT?
choices: the smallest $C\ge 0$ with $\|Tx\|\le C\|x\|$ for all $x$
questions.explain: The infimum is $0$ for any linear $T$ (take $x=0$). The first three expressions are all standard equivalent formulas for the operator norm.

questions.q: On $\mathbb{R}^2$ with Euclidean norm, what is the operator norm of the diagonal matrix $T=\mathrm{diag}(3,-4)$?
questions.explain: For a real diagonal matrix, $\|T\|=\max_i|t_i|$ since the singular values are $|t_i|$. Here $\max(3,4)=4$.

questions.q: Compute the operator norm of $T=\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\2&1\end{pmatrix}$ (symmetric, so $\|T\|=\max|\lambda_i|$).
questions.explain: Eigenvalues of the symmetric matrix satisfy $(1-\lambda)^2-4=0$, so $\lambda=3$ or $\lambda=-1$. The operator norm is $\max(|3|,|-1|)=3$.

hard.q: $B(X,Y)$ is a Banach space whenever $Y$ is a Banach space — even if $X$ is merely normed. Which step of the proof of completeness of $B(X,Y)$ is MOST subtle?
choices: Showing that a Cauchy $(T_n)$ in $B(X,Y)$ defines a pointwise limit $Tx=\lim T_n x$ in $Y$, verifying linearity, and then showing $\|T_n-T\|\to 0$ in operator norm (not just pointwise)
Applying Banach–Alaoglu to the unit ball
hard.hint: The nonobvious step is operator-norm convergence, not pointwise convergence.
hard.explain: Pointwise convergence $T_n x\to Tx$ is given by completeness of $Y$ and the Cauchy property of $(T_n x)$. Upgrading to operator-norm convergence uses a uniform $\varepsilon$ over $\|x\|\le 1$: for $n,m\ge N$, $\|T_n x-T_m x\|\le\varepsilon\|x\|$; let $m\to\infty$ to get $\|T_n x-Tx\|\le\varepsilon\|x\|$, so $\|T_n-T\|\le\varepsilon$.

hard.q: Let $T=\begin{pmatrix}3&4\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ (Euclidean). Compute $\|T\|$ via the largest singular value.
hard.hint: Compute $T^\top T$ and find its largest eigenvalue, then take square root.
hard.explain: $T^\top T=\begin{pmatrix}9&12\\12&16\end{pmatrix}$ has eigenvalues $25$ and $0$. Largest singular value is $\sqrt{25}=5$. Matches: the single nonzero row has norm $\sqrt{9+16}=5$.

hard.q: Which operator on $\ell^2$ satisfies $\|T\|=1$ yet has $T^n\to 0$ in the strong operator topology (i.e. $T^n x\to 0$ for every $x$), while $\|T^n\|=1$ for all $n$?
hard.explain: $S^*$ (left shift) has $\|S^*\|=1$ and $\|(S^*)^n\|=1$ for all $n$, yet $(S^*)^n x\to 0$ for every $x\in\ell^2$ since only the tail past index $n$ remains. This is the prototypical "strongly convergent but not uniformly convergent" example, showing operator-norm convergence is strictly stronger than pointwise/strong.

questions.q: For a linear map $T\colon X\to Y$ between normed spaces, which of the following is NOT equivalent to the others?
questions.explain: Continuity at a point, continuity everywhere, and boundedness are equivalent for linear maps. Injectivity is unrelated: the zero map is bounded and continuous but not injective.

questions.q: The adjoint $T^*$ of a bounded operator on a Hilbert space satisfies the $C^*$-identity:
questions.explain: The defining $C^*$-identity is $\|T^*T\|=\|T\|^2$. Combined with $\|T^*\|=\|T\|$, it pins down the Hilbert-space operator norm.

questions.q: On $\ell^2$, let $S$ be the unilateral (right) shift $S(x_1,x_2,\ldots)=(0,x_1,x_2,\ldots)$. What is the operator norm $\|S\|$?
questions.explain: $S$ is an isometry: $\|Sx\|_2=\|x\|_2$ for every $x\in\ell^2$, so $\|S\|=1$.

hard.q: Let $S$ be the unilateral right shift on $\ell^2$ and $S^*$ its adjoint (left shift). Which statement about their composition is correct?
choices: $S^*S=I$ but $SS^*\ne I$; in fact $SS^*=I-P$ where $P$ is the projection onto $e_1$
hard.hint: One composition recovers $x$; the other first drops then reinserts a coordinate.
hard.explain: $S^*$ is the left shift: $S^*(x_1,x_2,\ldots)=(x_2,x_3,\ldots)$. Then $S^*S(x)=S^*(0,x_1,x_2,\ldots)=(x_1,x_2,\ldots)=x$, so $S^*S=I$. But $(SS^*)(x_1,x_2,\ldots)=S(x_2,x_3,\ldots)=(0,x_2,x_3,\ldots)$, missing the first coordinate — so $SS^*=I-P_{e_1}$. $S$ is an isometry but not unitary: it is not surjective.

hard.q: The Open Mapping Theorem requires both domain and codomain to be Banach spaces. Which example shows the theorem can fail if the domain is not complete?
choices: The identity map $\mathrm{id}:(\mathbb{R}^n,\|\cdot\|_1)\to(\mathbb{R}^n,\|\cdot\|_2)$ is bijective and bounded but not open
The inclusion $i:(c_{00},\|\cdot\|_2)\hookrightarrow\ell^2$ is surjective and bounded but $i(B_{c_{00}})$ is not open in $\ell^2$
Every bijective bounded linear map between normed spaces has a bounded inverse
Differentiation $d/dx:C^1[0,1]\to C[0,1]$ is surjective and open
hard.explain: $c_{00}$ (finitely supported sequences) with the $\ell^2$ norm is a normed space but not complete. The inclusion into $\ell^2$ is bounded and injective with dense range; a surjective bounded map from an incomplete space to a Banach space need not be open, so the inverse need not be bounded. The Open Mapping Theorem requires the domain to be Banach.

hard.q: Let $T$ be the $2\times 2$ matrix $\begin{pmatrix}3&4\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$ viewed as an operator $\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$ with the Euclidean norm. Compute $\|T\|$ (the operator norm), which equals the largest singular value of $T$.
hard.explain: The singular values of $T$ are the square roots of the eigenvalues of $T^\top T=\begin{pmatrix}9&12\\12&16\end{pmatrix}$. Its eigenvalues are $25$ and $0$, so the largest singular value — and hence the operator norm — is $\sqrt{25}=5$.

questions.q: On a Hilbert space $H$, the adjoint $T^*$ of $T\in B(H)$ is the unique operator satisfying:
questions.explain: The defining identity of the adjoint is $\langle Tx,y\rangle=\langle x,T^*y\rangle$. Existence and uniqueness follow from the Riesz representation theorem applied to $y\mapsto\langle Tx,y\rangle$.

questions.q: Select all matrices that are self-adjoint (Hermitian) as operators on $\mathbb{C}^2$.
questions.explain: All three Pauli matrices are Hermitian (self-adjoint): $\sigma_k^*=\sigma_k$. The skew-symmetric matrix $J=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\-1&0\end{pmatrix}$ satisfies $J^*=-J$, so it is anti-self-adjoint, not self-adjoint.

questions.q: For $T=\begin{pmatrix}2&i\\ 3&0\end{pmatrix}$ on $\mathbb{C}^2$, compute $(T^*)_{12}$ (the top-right entry of the adjoint). Give your answer as a real number (it happens to be real here).
questions.explain: The adjoint is the conjugate transpose: $T^*=\begin{pmatrix}\bar 2&\bar 3\\ \overline{i}&\bar 0\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}2&3\\-i&0\end{pmatrix}$. So $(T^*)_{12}=3$.

hard.q: Classify the operator $U=\frac{1}{\sqrt 2}\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\1&-1\end{pmatrix}$ (Hadamard matrix) on $\mathbb{R}^2$.
choices: both self-adjoint AND unitary (so $U^2=I$ and spectrum $\subset\{\pm 1\}$)
normal but neither self-adjoint nor unitary
hard.explain: $U^\top=U$ (real symmetric, hence self-adjoint). Also $U^\top U=\tfrac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix}2&0\\0&2\end{pmatrix}=I$, so $U$ is unitary. A self-adjoint unitary is called a reflection: $U^2=I$, eigenvalues $\pm 1$.

hard.q: Match each operator class to the defining identity between $T$ and $T^*$.
hard.explain: Self-adjoint: $T^*=T$. Skew-adjoint: $T^*=-T$. Normal: $T^*T=TT^*$ (strictly weaker — includes self-adjoint and unitary). Unitary: $T^*T=TT^*=I$ (i.e., $T$ is an isometric bijection).

hard.q: On $L^2[0,1]$, consider the Volterra operator $(Vf)(x)=\int_0^x f(t)\,dt$. What is its adjoint $V^*$?
hard.explain: Change the order of integration: $\langle Vf,g\rangle=\int_0^1\int_0^x f(t)g(x)\,dt\,dx=\int_0^1 f(t)\int_t^1 g(x)\,dx\,dt=\langle f,V^*g\rangle$, so $(V^*g)(t)=\int_t^1 g(x)\,dx$. Volterra is NOT self-adjoint; in fact $V+V^*$ is self-adjoint and equals the rank-one operator $(Mf)(x)=\int_0^1 f$ (a constant function).

questions.q: Match each of the big four theorems to its tagline.
left: functional on subspace extends to whole space with same norm
surjective bounded linear map between Banach spaces is open
closed-graph linear maps between Banach spaces are bounded
pointwise bounded family of operators is uniformly bounded
questions.hint: Hahn–Banach extends functionals; Open Mapping gives openness (so inverses of bijections are bounded); Closed Graph turns a a-priori estimate into continuity; Banach–Steinhaus (uniform boundedness)…
questions.explain: Hahn–Banach extends functionals; Open Mapping gives openness (so inverses of bijections are bounded); Closed Graph turns a a-priori estimate into continuity; Banach–Steinhaus (uniform boundedness) upgrades pointwise bounds to norm bounds.

questions.q: Three of the big four theorems are proved via the Baire category theorem. Which one is NOT?
questions.explain: Hahn–Banach rests on Zorn's lemma (or the axiom of choice), not on Baire category. The other three all use the Baire category theorem on a Banach space — a complete metric space is not a countable union of nowhere-dense sets.

questions.q: A direct consequence of Hahn–Banach on a normed space $X$ is that $X^*$ separates points, i.e., for every $x\ne 0$ there is $\ell\in X^*$ with $\ell(x)\ne 0$. What is the standard one-line construction?
choices: Define $\ell$ on the line $\mathbb{K}x$ by $\ell(\alpha x)=\alpha\|x\|$ (so $\|\ell\|=1$ there) and extend by Hahn–Banach
Use the Riesz representation theorem, then restrict to $X$
questions.explain: On the 1-dimensional subspace $\mathbb{K}x$, define $\ell_0(\alpha x)=\alpha\|x\|$ so that $|\ell_0|=\|\cdot\|$ there. Hahn–Banach extends $\ell_0$ to $\ell\in X^*$ with $\|\ell\|=1$ and $\ell(x)=\|x\|>0$.

hard.q: The Closed Graph Theorem is often used contrapositively: if $T\colon X\to Y$ is linear between Banach spaces but UNbounded, then its graph is not closed. What does this translate to concretely?
choices: There exist $x_n\to 0$ in $X$ with $Tx_n\to y\ne 0$ in $Y$ — convergence is 'lost' under $T$
The unit ball of $X$ is unbounded
hard.hint: Graph-closedness says: if both $x_n\to x$ and $Tx_n\to y$, then $y=Tx$. Failure means $y$ exists but $y\ne Tx$.
hard.explain: If the graph is not closed, there is a sequence with $x_n\to x$ and $Tx_n\to y$ but $y\ne Tx$. Taking $x=0$ gives $x_n\to 0$ with $Tx_n\to y\ne T(0)=0$: a bounded $T$ cannot do this. Example: differentiation on $C[0,1]\to C[0,1]$ with domain $C^1[0,1]$ — $f_n(x)=\sin(n^2 x)/n\to 0$ uniformly but $f_n'\to$ nothing uniformly; graph is not closed in $C[0,1]\times C[0,1]$.

hard.q: A classical application of Banach–Steinhaus (the uniform boundedness principle): there exists a continuous $2\pi$-periodic function whose Fourier series diverges at a dense set of points. Which functional is 'resonating' in the proof?
choices: the $N$-th partial-sum evaluated at $0$: $L_N(f)=S_N f(0)$ on $C(\mathbb{T})$, whose norm is the $N$-th Lebesgue constant $\|L_N\|\sim\log N\to\infty$
hard.explain: Banach–Steinhaus says: if $\sup_N\|L_N\|=\infty$, then $\{f:\sup_N|L_N(f)|<\infty\}$ is meager. The Lebesgue constants $\|L_N\|=\int_{-\pi}^\pi|D_N(t)|dt/(2\pi)$ grow like $\log N$, so the set of $f$ with $S_N f(0)$ bounded is meager — and for generic $f$, the Fourier series diverges at $0$. By translation, divergence occurs on a dense set.

hard.q: Prove: if $T\colon X\to Y$ is a bounded linear bijection between Banach spaces, then $T^{-1}$ is bounded. Which step correctly continues after step 2?
steps: 1. $T$ is bounded linear and bijective between Banach spaces.
2. By the Open Mapping Theorem, $T$ is an open map (image of every open set is open).
choices: The map $T^{-1}\colon Y\to X$ is continuous iff the preimage of every open set under $T^{-1}$ is open, i.e., iff $T$ sends open sets to open sets — which we just established in step 2.
Observe that $T$ has closed range; conclude by Closed Graph.
hard.explain: Step 3 unpacks: $T^{-1}$ continuous $\iff (T^{-1})^{-1}(U)=T(U)$ is open for every open $U$ — exactly openness of $T$. So openness of $T$ (step 2) IS the boundedness of $T^{-1}$. This is the Inverse Mapping Theorem as a corollary of the Open Mapping Theorem.

questions.q: A bounded operator $T\colon X\to Y$ is compact if:
choices: $T$ maps bounded sets to relatively compact sets (equivalently, bounded sequences to sequences with norm-convergent subsequences)
questions.explain: A compact operator sends the unit ball (bounded) to a relatively compact (precompact) set. Equivalently, every bounded sequence $(x_n)$ has $(Tx_{n_k})$ norm-convergent. This is far stronger than continuity.

questions.q: Select all operators that are compact on an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space.
questions.explain: The identity is not compact on an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space (the unit ball is not compact — Riesz's lemma). Finite-rank operators are always compact. Diagonal operators with $\lambda_n\to 0$ are compact (norm-limit of finite-rank truncations). $\lambda_n=1+1/n\to 1\ne 0$, so not compact.

questions.q: The Fredholm alternative says: for compact $T$ and $\lambda\ne 0$, exactly one of the following holds for $(T-\lambda I)x=y$:
choices: a solution always exists for every $y$
either $(T-\lambda I)$ is bijective (unique solution for every $y$), or $\ker(T-\lambda I)\ne\{0\}$ and solutions exist iff $y\perp\ker(T^*-\bar\lambda I)$
there is never a solution unless $y=0$
solutions exist iff $y$ is an eigenvector of $T$
questions.hint: Fredholm alternative dichotomy: for $\lambda\ne 0$ in $\sigma(T)\cup\rho(T)$, either $T-\lambda I$ is a bijection, or $\dim\ker(T-\lambda I)=\dim\mathrm{coker}(T-\lambda I)<\infty$ and solvability is…
questions.explain: Fredholm alternative dichotomy: for $\lambda\ne 0$ in $\sigma(T)\cup\rho(T)$, either $T-\lambda I$ is a bijection, or $\dim\ker(T-\lambda I)=\dim\mathrm{coker}(T-\lambda I)<\infty$ and solvability is equivalent to a finite family of orthogonality conditions.

hard.q: Which statement about the spectrum of a compact operator $T$ on an infinite-dimensional Banach space is FALSE?
choices: every nonzero $\lambda\in\sigma(T)$ is an eigenvalue with finite-dimensional generalized eigenspace
$\sigma(T)$ is at most countable with $0$ as the only possible accumulation point
hard.hint: Compact operators can have two distinct isolated nonzero eigenvalues.
hard.explain: The spectrum of a compact operator is typically disconnected: a discrete sequence $\{\lambda_n\}_n$ accumulating only at $0$, together with $0$ itself. For instance, $T=\mathrm{diag}(1,1/2,1/3,\ldots)$ on $\ell^2$ has $\sigma(T)=\{0,1,1/2,1/3,\ldots\}$, highly disconnected.

hard.q: Rellich's theorem: the inclusion $H^1_0(\Omega)\hookrightarrow L^2(\Omega)$ for a bounded domain $\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ is compact. Combined with the spectral theorem, what does this give for the Dirichlet Laplacian $-\Delta$ on $\Omega$?
choices: An orthonormal basis $\{\phi_k\}$ of $L^2(\Omega)$ with $-\Delta\phi_k=\lambda_k\phi_k$ and $0<\lambda_1\le\lambda_2\le\cdots\to\infty$
hard.explain: The resolvent $(-\Delta)^{-1}$ (Green's operator) is bounded $L^2\to H^1_0$ by Lax–Milgram, and $H^1_0\hookrightarrow L^2$ is compact by Rellich. So $(-\Delta)^{-1}$ is compact self-adjoint on $L^2$; Hilbert–Schmidt gives an ONB of eigenfunctions with eigenvalues of $(-\Delta)^{-1}$ accumulating at $0$, i.e. eigenvalues of $-\Delta$ tending to $+\infty$.

hard.q: Consider the diagonal operator $T$ on $\ell^2$ with $T e_n=\frac{1}{n}e_n$. Compute the Hilbert–Schmidt norm $\|T\|_{HS}^2=\sum_n\|Te_n\|^2=\sum_n 1/n^2$. Give the exact value $\pi^2/6$ numerically.
hard.explain: $\|T\|_{HS}^2=\sum_{n=1}^\infty 1/n^2=\pi^2/6\approx 1.6449$. Since the HS norm is finite, $T$ is Hilbert–Schmidt (hence compact). By contrast, the trace norm $\|T\|_1=\sum 1/n=\infty$, so $T$ is NOT trace-class — the hierarchy trace-class $\subsetneq$ HS $\subsetneq$ compact is strict.

questions.q: The spectrum of a self-adjoint bounded operator $T$ on a Hilbert space is always:
choices: a subset of the unit circle $\{|z|=1\}$
questions.explain: Self-adjoint operators have real spectrum. Unitary operators have spectrum on the unit circle; bounded operator spectra on complex Hilbert spaces are always nonempty and compact.

questions.q: The multiplication form of the spectral theorem says every bounded self-adjoint $T$ on a separable Hilbert space is unitarily equivalent to:
choices: multiplication by a real $g\in L^\infty(X,\mu)$ on $L^2(X,\mu)$
questions.explain: After a unitary change of coordinates, every bounded self-adjoint operator becomes multiplication by a bounded real-valued function on an $L^2$ space.

questions.q: Let $T=\begin{pmatrix}0&-i\\ i&0\end{pmatrix}=\sigma_y$ acting on $\mathbb{C}^2$. Its spectrum is $\{-1,+1\}$. Give the product of its two eigenvalues as $a+bi$.
questions.explain: The Pauli matrix $\sigma_y$ has eigenvalues $+1$ and $-1$; their product equals $\det(\sigma_y)=-1=-1+0i$, matching the general fact $\det T=\prod\lambda_i$.

hard.q: Which is the correct sequence of steps to prove that eigenvalues of a self-adjoint operator $T$ on a Hilbert space are real?
choices: Assume $Tv=\lambda v$, $v\ne 0$ → compute $\langle Tv,v\rangle=\lambda\|v\|^2$ → use $T=T^*$ to get $\langle Tv,v\rangle=\langle v,Tv\rangle=\overline{\lambda}\|v\|^2$ → conclude $\lambda=\overline{\lambda}$
Use the spectral theorem to diagonalise $T$ → read off real diagonal entries
Apply the Cayley transform $U=(T-i)(T+i)^{-1}$ → note $U$ is unitary → eigenvalues of $U$ on the unit circle → invert to get $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$
Invoke Banach–Steinhaus to bound $\lambda$ → use the closed graph theorem to force $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$
hard.explain: The elementary proof: $\langle Tv,v\rangle=\lambda\|v\|^2$ from the eigenvalue equation, and $\langle Tv,v\rangle=\langle v,T^*v\rangle=\langle v,Tv\rangle=\overline{\lambda}\|v\|^2$ from self-adjointness. So $\lambda=\overline{\lambda}$, i.e., $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$. The spectral theorem proof is circular (it uses real spectrum as input).

hard.q: Let $M_g$ be the multiplication operator $(M_g f)(x)=g(x)f(x)$ on $L^2[0,1]$ with $g(x)=x$. Which statement about its spectrum is correct?
choices: $\sigma(M_g)=[0,1]$ — the entire range of $g$, and $M_g$ has no eigenvalues
$\sigma(M_g)=\mathbb{R}$ — multiplication operators always have full real spectrum
hard.explain: $M_g-\lambda$ is invertible iff $g-\lambda$ is bounded away from $0$ a.e., i.e., $\lambda\notin\operatorname{ess-range}(g)=[0,1]$. So $\sigma(M_g)=[0,1]$. For any $\lambda\in[0,1]$, $M_g-\lambda$ is injective (the set $\{x=\lambda\}$ has measure $0$), so $\lambda$ is not an eigenvalue — yet $\lambda$ is in the spectrum because $M_g-\lambda$ is not bounded below.

hard.q: The spectral projections of $T=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}$ onto eigenspaces $\lambda=+1$ and $\lambda=-1$ are $P_+=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$ and $P_-=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix}$. Compute $\|T\|^2=\|T^*T\|$ using the spectral decomposition $T=P_+-P_-$.

questions.q: For $T\in B(X)$ and $\lambda\in\sigma(T)$, classify $\lambda$ by WHY $T-\lambda I$ fails to be invertible. Match each row to its definition.
left: $T-\lambda I$ injective with range dense but not closed
$T-\lambda I$ injective with range not dense
questions.explain: Point = $\lambda$ is an eigenvalue. Continuous = injective, dense range, not closed (think multiplication by $x$ on $L^2[0,1]$). Residual = injective, range not dense (classic: $\lambda=0$ for the right shift).

questions.q: On $\ell^2$, let $S$ be the right shift. What is $\sigma_p(S)$ (point spectrum)?
questions.explain: If $Sx=\lambda x$ with $x\ne 0$, then $(0,x_1,x_2,\ldots)=\lambda(x_1,x_2,\ldots)$ gives $\lambda x_1=0$ and $\lambda x_{n+1}=x_n$. If $\lambda=0$, then $x=0$; if $\lambda\ne 0$, then $x_1=0$ and inductively $x=0$. So $S$ has no eigenvalues — $\sigma_p(S)=\emptyset$, yet $\sigma(S)=\overline{D(0,1)}$.

questions.q: The RESOLVENT set $\rho(T)$ and the spectrum $\sigma(T)$ partition $\mathbb{C}$. Which is TRUE?
choices: $\rho(T)$ is open and $\sigma(T)$ is compact; moreover $\sigma(T)\subset\{|\lambda|\le\|T\|\}$
questions.explain: The resolvent set is open (perturbing $T-\lambda I$ slightly keeps invertibility by a Neumann-series argument), so the spectrum is closed. It is also bounded by $\|T\|$ (for $|\lambda|>\|T\|$, the Neumann series $\sum(T/\lambda)^n/\lambda$ inverts $T-\lambda I$). Hence $\sigma(T)$ is compact, non-empty, and countable iff dictated by extra structure (e.g., compactness of $T$).

hard.q: On $\ell^2$, the right shift $S$ and the left shift $S^*$. Which statement correctly classifies $\lambda=0$ in each spectrum?
choices: $0$ is in the point spectrum of both, since the kernels are both $\{0\}$
$0\in\sigma_r(S)$ (residual) because $S$ is injective with range $\{x:x_1=0\}$ (not dense in $\ell^2$); and $0\in\sigma_p(S^*)$ (point) since $S^*e_1=0$
$0$ is in the continuous spectrum of both
hard.hint: Compute the range of $S$ and the kernel of $S^*$ separately.
hard.explain: $S$ is injective but $\mathrm{ran}(S)=\{x\in\ell^2:x_1=0\}$ is closed but not dense, so $0\in\sigma_r(S)$. $S^*e_1=0$, so $e_1$ is an eigenvector of $S^*$ with eigenvalue $0$, giving $0\in\sigma_p(S^*)$. This is the canonical example where residual spectrum of $T$ = conjugate of point spectrum of $T^*$.

hard.q: For self-adjoint $T$ on a Hilbert space, which pieces of the spectrum CAN be nonempty?
choices: point and continuous only (residual is empty)
hard.explain: A theorem: self-adjoint operators have empty residual spectrum. Proof: if $\lambda\in\sigma_r(T)$ (real, since $\sigma(T)\subset\mathbb{R}$), then $\mathrm{ran}(T-\lambda)^\perp=\ker(T^*-\lambda)^*=\ker(T-\lambda)\ne\{0\}$, forcing $\lambda\in\sigma_p(T)$ — contradiction. So for self-adjoint $T$, spectrum splits into point $\cup$ continuous.

hard.q: On $L^2[0,1]$, the multiplication operator $(M_x f)(x)=xf(x)$ has $\sigma(M_x)=[0,1]$. What is the Lebesgue measure of its POINT spectrum $\sigma_p(M_x)$?
hard.explain: $M_x$ has no eigenvalues: $(M_x-\lambda)f=0$ forces $f(x)=0$ for a.e. $x\ne\lambda$, so $f=0$ in $L^2$. Thus $\sigma_p(M_x)=\emptyset$, and its measure is $0$. The entire spectrum $[0,1]$ is continuous spectrum: $M_x-\lambda$ is injective with dense range but not surjective for $\lambda\in[0,1]$.

questions.q: The Riesz representation theorem on a Hilbert space $H$ says every $\ell\in H^*$ has the form $\ell(x)=\langle x,y\rangle$ for a unique $y\in H$. The map $y\mapsto\ell$ is:
questions.explain: Over $\mathbb{C}$, the map is conjugate-linear because $\langle x,\alpha y\rangle=\bar\alpha\langle x,y\rangle$; it is an isometric bijection, giving the canonical antilinear identification $H\cong H^*$.

questions.q: For $1<p<\infty$ and $\tfrac1p+\tfrac1q=1$ on a $\sigma$-finite measure space, the dual $(L^p)^*$ is isometrically identified with:
choices: the space of finite signed measures
questions.hint: The pairing $\langle f,g\rangle=\int fg\,d\mu$ is bounded by Hölder with constant $\|g\|_q$, and every bounded functional on $L^p$ arises this way — this is the content of the Riesz representation…
questions.explain: The pairing $\langle f,g\rangle=\int fg\,d\mu$ is bounded by Hölder with constant $\|g\|_q$, and every bounded functional on $L^p$ arises this way — this is the content of the Riesz representation for $L^p$.

questions.q: On $\ell^2$ let $\ell(x)=2x_1+x_2-2x_3$ for $x=(x_1,x_2,x_3,0,\ldots)$. By Riesz, $\ell(x)=\langle x,y\rangle$ for some $y\in\ell^2$. Compute $\|\ell\|=\|y\|_2$.
questions.explain: The representing vector is $y=(2,1,-2,0,\ldots)$ (real, so no conjugation), and $\|y\|_2=\sqrt{4+1+4}=\sqrt{9}=3$. Riesz makes $\|\ell\|_{H^*}=\|y\|_H$.

hard.q: The Riesz theorem fails for $p=1$: $(L^1)^*\cong L^\infty$ but $(L^\infty)^*\not\cong L^1$. Which is the standard counterexample showing $(L^\infty[0,1])^*$ is strictly larger than $L^1[0,1]$?
choices: The norm functional $f\mapsto\|f\|_{L^\infty}$ is in $(L^\infty)^*$ but is not given by any $L^1$ function
Evaluation at a point, e.g., $\delta_0(f)=f(0)$, is a bounded linear functional on $L^\infty[0,1]$ (choosing measurable representatives) but lies outside the image of $L^1$
The identity map $L^\infty\to L^\infty$ is a bounded functional not represented by any $g\in L^1$
The zero functional is in $(L^\infty)^*$ but not in $L^1$
hard.explain: Point evaluations are well-defined on $L^\infty$ (up to measure-zero sets they can be realized via limits and Banach limits) and give bounded linear functionals. The Banach limit — a positive linear functional on $\ell^\infty$ extending $\lim$ from convergent sequences — is the classical example: it is bounded, not $w^*$-continuous, and lies strictly outside the image of $\ell^1\hookrightarrow(\ell^\infty)^*$.

hard.q: Prove step-by-step that the Riesz vector $y$ in $\ell(x)=\langle x,y\rangle$ is unique. Which argument is correct?
choices: Suppose $\langle x,y_1\rangle=\langle x,y_2\rangle$ for all $x$ → then $\langle x,y_1-y_2\rangle=0$ for all $x$ → set $x=y_1-y_2$ → get $\|y_1-y_2\|^2=0$ → so $y_1=y_2$
Use Gram–Schmidt to orthogonalise $y_1,y_2$; if they span the same functional they must coincide
Apply Banach–Alaoglu to extract a unique weak-* limit
Uniqueness follows from the Hahn–Banach theorem applied to the subspace spanned by $y_1-y_2$
hard.explain: The standard one-line uniqueness argument: if $\langle x,y_1\rangle=\langle x,y_2\rangle$ for all $x$, then $y:=y_1-y_2$ satisfies $\langle x,y\rangle=0$ for all $x$; taking $x=y$ gives $\|y\|^2=0$, forcing $y=0$. Existence uses the projection theorem onto a closed subspace.

hard.q: On $L^2[0,1]$, the functional $\ell(f)=\int_0^1 (3x^2-1)f(x)\,dx$ is represented by $y(x)=3x^2-1$. Compute $\|\ell\|=\|y\|_{L^2}$ to three decimal places (the answer is $\sqrt{\int_0^1(3x^2-1)^2dx}$).

questions.q: A sequence $(x_n)$ in a Banach space $X$ converges weakly to $x$ when:
questions.explain: Weak convergence $x_n\rightharpoonup x$ means $\ell(x_n)\to\ell(x)$ for every bounded linear functional $\ell$. Norm convergence is strictly stronger.

questions.q: The Banach–Alaoglu theorem states that the closed unit ball of $X^*$ is:
questions.explain: Banach–Alaoglu: $B_{X^*}$ is compact in the weak-* topology, proved by embedding it in the product $\prod_{x\in X}\overline{D(0,\|x\|)}$ and applying Tychonoff. Norm-compactness of the unit ball fails in every infinite-dimensional Banach space.

questions.q: In $\ell^2$, let $e_n$ be the $n$-th standard basis vector. The sequence $(e_n)$ does NOT converge in norm, but does converge weakly to a unique limit $w\in\ell^2$. Compute $\|w\|_2$.
questions.explain: For each $y\in\ell^2$, $\langle e_n,y\rangle=\overline{y_n}\to 0$, so $e_n\rightharpoonup 0$. Yet $\|e_n-0\|_2=1$, so there is no norm convergence — a textbook illustration that weak limit $\ne$ strong limit.

hard.q: Mazur's theorem states that for a convex set $C$ in a Banach space, weak closure equals norm closure. Which consequence is most directly useful in optimization?
choices: Every weakly closed set is bounded
If a sequence $x_n\rightharpoonup x$ weakly and each $x_n\in C$ (convex, norm-closed), then $x\in C$ — so norm-closed convex sets are closed under weak limits
Every norm-bounded sequence has a weakly convergent subsequence
Weak convergence implies norm convergence for convex sequences
hard.explain: Mazur: for convex $C$, weak-closure $=$ norm-closure. The key corollary: if $C$ is convex and norm-closed, it is also weakly closed, so weak limits of sequences in $C$ remain in $C$. This is indispensable in calculus of variations — one finds minimizers as weak limits in a reflexive space, then uses convexity to stay inside feasible sets.

hard.q: Let $X=\ell^1$ and consider its dual $X^*=\ell^\infty$. A sequence $(\ell_n)$ in $B_{\ell^\infty}$ converges weak-* to $\ell$ iff:
choices: $\ell_n(x)\to\ell(x)$ for every $x\in\ell^1$, i.e., $\sum_k (\ell_n)_k x_k\to\sum_k \ell_k x_k$ for all $x\in\ell^1$
$(\ell_n)$ is eventually constant in every coordinate
hard.explain: Weak-* convergence in $X^*=\ell^\infty$ is convergence on every $x\in X=\ell^1$. Norm convergence (choice A) is strictly stronger. Convergence on all of $\ell^\infty$ (choice C) would be weak convergence in $\ell^\infty$ itself — a finer topology. Concretely, $\delta_n\rightharpoonup^* 0$ in $\ell^\infty$ since $\delta_n(x)=x_n\to 0$ for every $x\in\ell^1$, but $\|\delta_n\|_\infty=1\not\to 0$.

hard.q: In $L^2[0,2\pi]$, consider $f_n(x)=\sin(nx)$. One can show $f_n\rightharpoonup 0$ weakly. Compute $\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_0^{2\pi}\sin(nx)\cdot x\,dx$ using integration by parts (this gives the value of the weak limit applied to $g(x)=x$, confirming $f_n\rightharpoonup 0$).
hard.explain: $\int_0^{2\pi} x\sin(nx)\,dx=\left[-\frac{x\cos(nx)}{n}\right]_0^{2\pi}+\frac{1}{n}\int_0^{2\pi}\cos(nx)\,dx=-\frac{2\pi}{n}+0\to 0$ as $n\to\infty$. Since this holds for all $g\in L^2[0,2\pi]$ (by Riemann–Lebesgue), $f_n\rightharpoonup 0$ weakly, even though $\|f_n\|_{L^2}=\sqrt{\pi}$ for all $n$.

questions.q: The canonical embedding $J\colon X\to X^{**}$ sends $x$ to the functional $Jx(\ell)=\ell(x)$. This map is always:
questions.explain: Hahn–Banach guarantees enough functionals to isometrically embed $X$ in $X^{**}$: $\|Jx\|=\sup_{\|\ell\|\le 1}|\ell(x)|=\|x\|$. $X$ is reflexive when $J$ is additionally surjective.

questions.q: Which of the following spaces are reflexive?
questions.explain: $\ell^p,L^p$ for $1<p<\infty$ and Hilbert spaces are reflexive. $L^1$ is not ($L^{1**}=L^{\infty *}\supsetneq L^1$). $C[0,1]$ is not (its dual is measures, whose dual is much larger). $\ell^\infty$ is not (its dual contains Banach limits outside $\ell^1$).

questions.q: An equivalent formulation of reflexivity (Kakutani): $X$ is reflexive iff its closed unit ball is:
questions.explain: Kakutani's theorem: $X$ is reflexive iff $B_X$ is weakly compact. Norm-compactness happens only in finite dimensions. Eberlein–Šmulian further says this weak compactness can be checked sequentially: $B_X$ weakly compact iff every bounded sequence has a weakly convergent subsequence.

hard.q: Why is $c_0$ (sequences tending to $0$, with sup norm) NOT reflexive? Pick the crispest reason.
choices: $c_0^{**}=\ell^\infty$ (since $c_0^*=\ell^1$ and $(\ell^1)^*=\ell^\infty$) strictly larger than $c_0$ — e.g., the constant sequence $(1,1,1,\ldots)\in\ell^\infty\setminus c_0$
hard.explain: The canonical chain $c_0\hookrightarrow c_0^{**}\cong\ell^\infty$ is strict because sequences not tending to $0$ (e.g., constants) lie in $\ell^\infty\setminus c_0$. So $J$ is not surjective. The sequence $(1,1,\ldots)\in\ell^\infty$ corresponds to a functional on $c_0^*=\ell^1$ that is NOT evaluation at any element of $c_0$.

hard.q: Which theorem about reflexive spaces is the foundation of the direct method in the calculus of variations?
choices: In a reflexive space, bounded sequences have weakly convergent subsequences (consequence of Banach–Alaoglu + Kakutani + Eberlein–Šmulian)
hard.explain: In a reflexive Banach space, the unit ball is weakly compact, so any bounded sequence has a weakly convergent subsequence. Coupling this with weak lower semicontinuity of a convex energy functional $F$ produces a minimizer: take a minimizing sequence, extract a weak limit $x^*$, and use $F(x^*)\le\liminf F(x_n)$.

hard.q: On any reflexive Banach space $X$, an additional regularity property holds automatically for every convex continuous function. Which one?
choices: attainment of the norm on any nonempty closed convex set by a (not necessarily unique) best approximation
hard.hint: Use weak compactness of bounded sets + weak lower semicontinuity of the norm.
hard.explain: For a nonempty closed convex $C\subset X$ reflexive and any $x\in X$, the infimum $\inf_{c\in C}\|x-c\|$ is attained. Take a minimizing sequence $(c_n)$; it is bounded so has a weakly convergent subsequence $c_{n_k}\rightharpoonup c^*\in C$ (Mazur + norm-closure of convex $C$); weak lower semicontinuity of the norm gives $\|x-c^*\|\le\liminf\|x-c_{n_k}\|$. Non-reflexive spaces generally lack this.

questions.q: A distribution on $\mathbb{R}$ is:
choices: a continuous linear functional on the space $C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R})$ of smooth compactly supported test functions
questions.explain: The space $\mathcal{D}'(\mathbb{R})=(C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R}))^*$ is, by definition, the continuous linear functionals on test functions (with a specific inductive-limit topology). Every locally integrable function $f$ defines a distribution $\phi\mapsto\int f\phi$, but distributions are strictly more general — they include $\delta$ and its derivatives.

questions.q: The Dirac delta distribution $\delta_0$ is defined by $\langle\delta_0,\phi\rangle=\phi(0)$. Which sequence of $L^1$ functions $f_n$ converges to $\delta_0$ in the distributional sense?
questions.explain: $f_n=n\cdot\mathbf{1}_{[0,1/n]}$ has $\int f_n=1$ and mass concentrating at $0$. For $\phi\in C_c^\infty$, $\int f_n\phi=n\int_0^{1/n}\phi(x)dx\to\phi(0)$ by continuity. Hence $f_n\to\delta_0$ in $\mathcal{D}'$.

questions.q: Compute $\langle\delta_0^{(1)},\phi\rangle$ where $\delta_0^{(1)}$ is the distributional derivative of $\delta_0$ and $\phi(x)=x^2-3x+5$. Recall $\langle\delta_0^{(1)},\phi\rangle=-\phi'(0)$.
questions.explain: Distributional differentiation: $\langle T',\phi\rangle=-\langle T,\phi'\rangle$. For $T=\delta_0$, $\langle\delta_0',\phi\rangle=-\langle\delta_0,\phi'\rangle=-\phi'(0)$. With $\phi(x)=x^2-3x+5$, $\phi'(x)=2x-3$, $\phi'(0)=-3$, so $-\phi'(0)=3$.

hard.q: The principal value distribution $\mathrm{p.v.}\,\tfrac{1}{x}$ is defined by $\langle\mathrm{p.v.}\tfrac{1}{x},\phi\rangle=\lim_{\varepsilon\to 0^+}\int_{|x|>\varepsilon}\phi(x)/x\,dx$. Which identity relates the distributional derivative of $\log|x|$ to it?
hard.hint: Pair the derivative with a test function and integrate by parts away from $0$, taking a symmetric limit.
hard.explain: For $\phi\in C_c^\infty$: $\langle(\log|x|)',\phi\rangle=-\langle\log|x|,\phi'\rangle=-\lim_{\varepsilon\to 0}\int_{|x|>\varepsilon}\log|x|\phi'(x)dx$. Integrate by parts; boundary terms $\log\varepsilon(\phi(\varepsilon)-\phi(-\varepsilon))\to 0$ because $\phi(\varepsilon)-\phi(-\varepsilon)=O(\varepsilon)$. The remaining integral is $\lim_{\varepsilon\to 0}\int_{|x|>\varepsilon}\phi(x)/x\,dx=\langle\mathrm{p.v.}\tfrac{1}{x},\phi\rangle$. The singularity at $0$ is regularized by the principal-value cancellation.

hard.q: Tempered distributions $\mathcal{S}'(\mathbb{R}^n)$ are the dual of Schwartz space $\mathcal{S}$ (rapidly decreasing smooth functions). Which property makes them the correct setting for Fourier analysis?
choices: The Fourier transform is a continuous automorphism of $\mathcal{S}$, so it extends by duality to an automorphism of $\mathcal{S}'$ — allowing $\hat{\delta}=1$, $\hat 1=(2\pi)^{n/2}\delta$, and Fourier transforms of polynomials
Every tempered distribution is a function
hard.explain: The Fourier transform $\mathcal{F}\colon\mathcal{S}\to\mathcal{S}$ is a topological isomorphism. By duality, $\hat{T}\in\mathcal{S}'$ is defined by $\langle\hat T,\phi\rangle=\langle T,\hat\phi\rangle$. This extends Fourier to objects with no Fourier integral as a function: $\delta$, polynomials, $\mathrm{p.v.}\tfrac{1}{x}$. It is the foundation of PDE via Fourier multipliers. Arbitrary distributions in $\mathcal{D}'$ do NOT have Fourier transforms in general.

hard.q: Prove that $x\cdot\delta_0=0$ as a distribution on $\mathbb{R}$. Pick the correct next step.
steps: 1. By definition, $(x\cdot\delta_0)$ acts on $\phi\in C_c^\infty$ as $\langle x\cdot\delta_0,\phi\rangle=\langle\delta_0,x\phi\rangle$ (multiplying a distribution by a smooth function is the dual of multiplying the test function).
choices: $(x\phi)(0)=0\cdot\phi(0)=0$ for every $\phi$, so $x\cdot\delta_0=0$ as a distribution.
hard.explain: The function $g(x)=x\phi(x)$ vanishes at $0$: $g(0)=0$. So $\langle x\delta_0,\phi\rangle=0$ for every $\phi$, which is the definition of the zero distribution. This identity $x\delta_0=0$ is the distributional version of the algebraic fact — and it is why $\delta_0$ is a genuine new object, not a function: no function $f$ satisfies $xf=0$ a.e. yet $\int f=1$.
